section.title: Elisha and the Aramean Raiders
section.sectionRationale: Two connected episodes show Elisha's power extending from a small domestic accident to international warfare. A borrowed axe head is recovered by a floating miracle, and then Elisha repeatedly foils the king of Aram's ambushes, prompting an attempt to capture him at Dothan. There the LORD opens a servant's eyes to see the unseen armies of heaven, strikes the Aramean force with blindness, and Elisha leads them safely into Samaria, where mercy rather than slaughter ends the raids.
section.thesis: The LORD who cares for the smallest lost tool also commands unseen armies of fire and blinds Israel's enemies, yet chooses mercy over slaughter when they are in Elisha's power, teaching Israel that true security lies not in weapons but in the God who sees and provides even when His people cannot.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the floating axe head, verses 1 to 7; Elisha foils Aram's ambushes, verses 8 to 14; and the blinded raiders shown mercy at Samaria, verses 15 to 23.

units.label: The axe head floats
units.note: oh my master, it was borrowed

verses.text: Now the sons of the prophets said to Elisha, "Please take note that the place where we meet with you is too small for us.
Please let us go to the Jordan, where each of us can get a log so we can build ourselves a place to live there." "Go," said Elisha.
Then one of them said, "Please come with your servants." "I will come," he replied.
So Elisha went with them, and when they came to the Jordan, they began to cut down some trees.
As one of them was cutting down a tree, the {{barzel|iron}} axe head fell into the water. "Oh, my master," he cried out, "it was borrowed!"
"Where did it fall?" asked the man of God. And when he showed him the place, the man of God cut a stick, threw it there, and made the iron float.
"Lift it out," he said, and the man reached out his hand and took it.

units.label: Elisha foils Aram's ambushes
units.note: the very words you speak in your bedroom

verses.text: Now the king of Aram was at war against Israel. After consulting with his servants, he said, "My camp will be in such and such a place."
Then the man of God sent word to the king of Israel: "Be careful not to pass by this place, for the Arameans are going down there."
So the king of Israel sent word to the place the man of God had pointed out. Time and again Elisha warned the king, so that he was on his guard in such places.
For this reason the king of Aram became enraged and called his servants to demand of them, "Tell me, which one of us is on the side of the king of Israel?"
But one of his servants replied, "No one, my lord the king. For Elisha, the prophet in Israel, tells the king of Israel the very words you speak in your bedroom."
So the king said, "Go and see where he is, that I may send men to capture him." On receiving the report, "Elisha is in Dothan,"
the king of Aram sent horses, chariots, and a great army. They went there by night and surrounded the city.

units.label: Eyes opened, eyes blinded
units.note: those who are with us are more than those who are with them

verses.text: When the servant of the man of God got up and went out early in the morning, behold, an army with horses and chariots had surrounded the city. So he asked Elisha, "Oh, my master, what are we to do?"
"Do not be afraid," Elisha answered, "for those who are with us are more than those who are with them."

Then Elisha prayed, "O LORD, please open his {{ayin|eyes}} that he may see." And the LORD opened the eyes of the young man, and he saw that the hills were full of horses and chariots of fire all around Elisha.
As the Arameans came down against him, Elisha prayed to the LORD, "Please strike these people with {{sanverim|blindness}}." So He struck them with blindness, according to the word of Elisha.
And Elisha told them, "This is not the way, and this is not the city. Follow me, and I will take you to the man you are seeking." And he led them to Samaria.

units.label: Mercy at Samaria
units.note: set food and water before them

verses.text: When they had entered Samaria, Elisha said, "O LORD, open the eyes of these men that they may see." Then the LORD opened their eyes, and they looked around and discovered that they were in Samaria.
And when the king of Israel saw them, he asked Elisha, "My father, shall I kill them? Shall I kill them?"

"Do not kill them," he replied. "Would you kill those you have captured with your own sword or bow? Set {{lechem|food}} and water before them, that they may eat and drink and then return to their master."

So the king prepared a great feast for them, and after they had finished eating and drinking, he sent them away, and they returned to their master. And the Aramean raiders did not come into the land of Israel again.

barzel.gloss: Iron, here the head of a borrowed axe lost in the Jordan. Iron tools were valuable and not easily replaced in this era, so the young prophet's distress, oh my master, it was borrowed, reflects a real and costly loss that Elisha treats as worth the LORD's attention.

ayin.gloss: Eye. Elisha's prayer, open his eyes, asks the LORD to let his servant see the unseen spiritual reality already surrounding them, chariots and horses of fire, teaching that the deepest problem in fear is often not the danger itself but blindness to God's greater, unseen presence.

sanverim.gloss: A rare Hebrew word for blindness or dazzling confusion, used elsewhere only of the men who tried to break into Lot's house, Genesis 19:11. It suggests not ordinary darkness but a supernatural disorientation, striking the army with helpless confusion rather than harm.

lechem.gloss: Bread or food generally. Elisha's command to feed captured enemy soldiers a great feast, rather than execute them, embodies remarkable covenant mercy, echoing Proverbs 25:21, and it directly produces the peace that follows, the Aramean raiders no longer came into Israel.

basic.context: Two episodes show the range of Elisha's ministry. First, when a young prophet loses a borrowed axe head in the Jordan, Elisha makes the iron float so it can be retrieved, a small mercy for an ordinary loss. Then, in the larger context of ongoing war with Aram, Elisha repeatedly warns the king of Israel of Aramean ambush locations, so that the frustrated king of Aram sends an army to capture the prophet at Dothan. When Elisha's servant panics at being surrounded, Elisha prays that his eyes be opened, revealing an unseen army of fire around them, then prays the enemy be struck with blindness, leads them safely into Samaria, and, refusing to let the king kill them, has them fed and released, ending the raids.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recounting real, specific events; it is not a promise that lost objects will always float back or that every conflict will be resolved by supernatural blindness. What endures is the truth that God cares about small losses as well as national security, that unseen spiritual realities surround His people even when they cannot perceive them, and that mercy toward a defeated enemy can accomplish more than violence.
basic.originalLanguages: A lost barzel, iron axe head, matters enough to Elisha to warrant a miracle. When his servant panics, Elisha prays that his ayin, eyes, be opened to see the fiery armies already present, and then that the Arameans be struck with sanverim, a rare word for supernatural blinding confusion. The chapter ends with lechem, bread, offered generously to a defeated enemy, mercy that succeeds where violence had failed to bring lasting peace.
basic.typology: The servant whose eyes are opened to see the armies of heaven surrounding God's people pictures the spiritual sight believers need to perceive God's unseen protection and provision, while the mercy shown to captured enemies anticipates the gospel's call to love enemies rather than destroy them.
basic.christ: Elisha's prayer to open blind eyes, revealing the greater reality of God's protecting presence, anticipates Christ, who opens spiritually blind eyes to see the kingdom of God, John 9:39. And Elisha's insistence on feeding rather than killing captured enemies embodies in advance Christ's own teaching to love your enemies and do good to those who hate you, Luke 6:27, ending hostility through mercy rather than force.
basic.god: God is attentive to the smallest need, a borrowed tool lost in a river, and sovereign over the largest threats, armies bent on capturing His prophet. He surrounds His people with unseen protection even when fear blinds them to it, and He is a God whose mercy, extended even to enemies, accomplishes what force alone cannot.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 2:12 shows chariots of fire also attending Elijah's departure, the same heavenly army revealed here around Elisha. Proverbs 25:21-22 commends feeding a hungry enemy, precisely what Elisha commands here. Romans 12:20-21 quotes that proverb directly, teaching believers to overcome evil with good, exactly the pattern this chapter displays.
basic.oneStory: From a lost axe head to a surrounded prophet to a defeated invading army, God shows Himself attentive, sovereign, and merciful at every scale, previewing the gospel truth that His people are never as alone or endangered as they appear, and that mercy toward enemies, ultimately fulfilled at the cross, accomplishes lasting peace that force cannot achieve.

context.literary: The chapter pairs two episodes of contrasting scale, a small domestic mercy and a national military crisis, united by Elisha's consistent character: attentive to real need, unafraid of danger because he perceives God's unseen resources, and merciful even toward enemies delivered into his power.
context.historical: Iron tools were costly and often borrowed among poorer communities like the sons of the prophets, making the lost axe head a genuine hardship. Ongoing border skirmishes between Israel and Aram, centered on Damascus, form the backdrop for the ambush and capture narrative, and Dothan and Samaria were real, identifiable locations in the northern kingdom.
context.canonical: This chapter continues the Elisha cycle's pattern, seen already in chapter 4, of God's care extending from ordinary domestic need to matters of state and war, and it anticipates the more severe siege of Samaria narrated in the following chapters.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the LORD who cares for the smallest loss also commands unseen heavenly forces and grants victory over Israel's enemies, while teaching through Elisha's mercy that God's power is meant to protect and reconcile, not merely to destroy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates specific historical miracles and a specific act of mercy in a particular war; it does not prescribe blinding enemies or guarantee that lost objects will be supernaturally recovered. What endures is confidence in God's unseen presence and the wisdom of responding to a defeated enemy with mercy rather than needless violence.

debates.question: Why did Elisha insist on mercy rather than allowing the king to kill the blinded Aramean soldiers, verses 21 to 22?

views.label: Mercy as a strategically wiser and more godly response than execution
views.case: Elisha points out these men were not captured in ordinary battle by Israel's own sword or bow but delivered by God's miraculous power, making their fate God's prerogative rather than a normal wartime execution; the resulting feast and release directly produced peace, the raids stopped, verse 23, validating mercy's wisdom.

views.label: An enacted picture of covenant kindness meant to shame and disarm an enemy
views.case: Feeding a defeated, helpless enemy rather than killing them reflects the wisdom of Proverbs 25:21-22, heaping coals of shame on the enemy through kindness, a deliberate strategy to end hostility through moral and relational means rather than further bloodshed.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that God's power delivered these men into Israel's hands specifically so that mercy, not vengeance, could be displayed, and this course of action directly achieved lasting peace from Aramean raids.

study.originalLanguages: A lost piece of barzel, iron, an ordinary borrowed tool, still matters enough for a miracle. When the servant's ayin, eyes, are opened by Elisha's prayer, he sees the LORD's fiery army already surrounding them, and when the enemy army is struck with sanverim, a rare and unusual word for disorienting blindness, they become entirely dependent on the very prophet they sought to capture. The chapter closes with lechem, bread, freely offered to defeated enemies, ending hostility through mercy rather than the sword.

typology.type: Eyes opened to see the unseen army of God surrounding His servant
typology.fulfillment: Spiritual sight granted through Christ, who opens blind eyes to perceive the kingdom of God and the reality of His protecting presence, John 9:39, 2 Corinthians 4:6.

typology.type: A defeated enemy shown mercy rather than destroyed
typology.fulfillment: Christ's command to love enemies and overcome evil with good, ultimately fulfilled at the cross where He prayed for those crucifying Him, Luke 23:34, Romans 12:20-21.

study.christ: Elisha's prayer that opens his servant's eyes to see the unseen armies of fire surrounding them anticipates Christ, who opens the eyes of the spiritually blind to see the kingdom of God present and near, John 9:39, so that fear gives way to faith in what cannot be seen with natural eyes. Elisha's refusal to let a captured, helpless enemy be killed, choosing instead to feed them and send them home in peace, embodies in advance the way of Christ, who taught His followers to love their enemies, Luke 6:27, and who Himself prayed for His executioners rather than calling down judgment, showing that mercy, not vengeance, is the path to true and lasting peace.
study.god: God is attentive at every scale, from the loss of a single borrowed tool to the movements of an invading army, showing that no need is too small for His concern and no threat too large for His power. He surrounds His people with unseen protection that fear alone cannot perceive, and His mercy, extended even to a captured enemy army, accomplishes lasting peace that force and violence could not secure.

crossReferences.note: Chariots of fire also attend Elijah's ascension, the same unseen heavenly army now revealed protecting Elisha at Dothan.
The only other biblical use of the rare word for blindness used here, describing the men at Lot's door struck with the same kind of supernatural disorientation.
Commends feeding a hungry enemy as a means of overcoming hostility, the very wisdom Elisha enacts in verses 22 to 23.
Paul directly quotes this proverb, teaching believers to overcome evil with good, the New Testament application of the principle displayed here.

study.oneStory: From a small lost tool to a surrounded prophet to an entire invading army, God proves Himself attentive, sovereign, and merciful at every scale, showing His people they are never as endangered or alone as fear suggests. The mercy Elisha shows a defeated enemy, ending years of raiding through kindness rather than the sword, anticipates the gospel's call to love enemies, a way of peace fulfilled completely at the cross of Jesus Christ.

quiz.q: How did Elisha resolve the loss of the borrowed axe head?

options.text: He cut a stick, threw it into the water where the axe head fell, and made the iron float so it could be retrieved.
options.note: Verses 5 to 7 record this exact sequence and outcome.

options.text: He told the young prophet to simply buy a replacement.
options.note: Elisha performed a miracle to recover the actual borrowed axe head rather than suggesting a replacement.

options.text: He sent divers into the Jordan to search for it.
options.note: No divers are mentioned; the axe head miraculously floated to the surface.

options.text: The axe head was never recovered.
options.note: The text explicitly states the man reached out and took it after it floated.

quiz.q: How did Elisha repeatedly protect Israel from the king of Aram's ambushes?

options.text: He warned the king of Israel of the exact locations the Arameans planned to camp, allowing Israel to avoid the ambushes.
options.note: Verses 9 to 10 record this repeated prophetic warning and its effect.

options.text: He personally led Israel's army into battle against Aram.
options.note: Elisha's role was prophetic warning, not military command, in this passage.

options.text: He negotiated a peace treaty directly with the king of Aram.
options.note: No treaty negotiation is described; Elisha's role was revealing the enemy's plans, prompting the king of Aram's anger rather than diplomacy.

options.text: He was unaware of Aram's plans and Israel suffered repeated losses.
options.note: The text states Elisha knew the plans precisely and warned the king, protecting Israel repeatedly.

quiz.q: What did Elisha's servant see after Elisha prayed for his eyes to be opened at Dothan?

options.text: The hills full of horses and chariots of fire surrounding Elisha, an army greater than the surrounding Aramean forces.
options.note: Verse 17 records exactly this vision after Elisha's prayer.

options.text: An empty landscape with no other forces present.
options.note: The text describes a vivid vision of a heavenly army, not an empty landscape.

options.text: The Aramean army retreating in fear before any prayer was offered.
options.note: The Arameans do not retreat at this point; Elisha instead prays for their blindness in the following verse.

options.text: A vision of the future fall of Samaria.
options.note: The vision granted was of the present protecting army of the LORD, not a future prophecy.

quiz.q: What happened to the Aramean army after they were struck with blindness at Dothan?

options.text: Elisha led them, disoriented, into Samaria itself, where their eyes were then opened and they discovered they were captured in the heart of Israel's territory.
options.note: Verses 18 to 20 record this exact sequence of blindness, leading, and revelation.

options.text: They immediately regained their sight and fled back to Aram.
options.note: The text describes them being led all the way into Samaria before their sight was restored, not an immediate escape.

options.text: They were slaughtered by the Israelite army while still blinded.
options.note: No slaughter occurs; Elisha specifically prevents the king of Israel from killing them.

options.text: They never entered Israelite territory at all.
options.note: The text explicitly states Elisha led them into Samaria, deep within Israel.

quiz.q: How did Elisha instruct the king of Israel to treat the captured Aramean soldiers, and what was the ultimate result?

options.text: He told the king to feed them a great feast and send them home rather than kill them, and afterward the Aramean raiders stopped coming into Israel.
options.note: Verses 22 to 23 record both the instruction and its lasting peaceful result.

options.text: He told the king to imprison them permanently as hostages.
options.note: Elisha instructed that they be fed and released, not imprisoned.

options.text: He told the king to kill them immediately as spoils of war.
options.note: Elisha explicitly forbade killing them, the opposite of this option.

options.text: The raids continued unabated despite Elisha's mercy.
options.note: The text explicitly states the Aramean raiders did not come into the land of Israel again.

questions: Elisha treated the loss of a simple borrowed tool as worth God's attention and a miracle. What small, seemingly insignificant loss or worry in your life do you need to bring to God rather than dismiss as too minor for Him to care about?

The servant panicked until his eyes were opened to see the armies already surrounding them. Where is fear currently blinding you to resources or protection God has already provided but you cannot yet see?

Elisha chose mercy, a feast, over violence when he had power over a defeated enemy. Where is God calling you to respond to someone who has wronged you with unexpected kindness rather than retaliation?

The mercy shown at Samaria accomplished more lasting peace than years of ambush and warfare had. What relationship in your life might be transformed more by grace than by continuing to fight for the upper hand?

Elisha lived with unusual calm in the face of real danger because he trusted what he could not yet see. What practice or discipline helps you cultivate that same kind of settled trust in God during uncertain seasons?

translationNotes.note: The sons of the prophets' complaint that their meeting place was too small reflects the ongoing growth of organized prophetic communities under Elisha's leadership after Elijah's departure.
The Hebrew word for blindness here, sanverim, is rare, appearing elsewhere only in Genesis 19:11, and likely describes a disorienting, dazzling confusion rather than ordinary physical darkness.

The BSB text notes the Aramean raiders did not come into the land of Israel again, marking Elisha's act of mercy as directly and lastingly effective in stopping this particular pattern of border raids.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 6:1-23. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
